title: PyBot Loops
description: Use for loops and nested loops to control PyBot. Progress from single commands to walking a full square perimeter with nested loops.

steps.title: Meet PyBot!
steps.content:
Meet PyBot! 🤖

PyBot is a robot you control using Python. Your job is to guide it to special tiles on the grid and light them up.

You have four commands:

walk_forward() — move one tile forward (only if it's the same height)
turn_right() — turn 90° clockwise
turn_left() — turn 90° anti-clockwise
light() — light up the tile you're standing on

⚠️ The level is complete when all glowing tiles have been lit. Each level shows how many lines of code you need for Gold 🥇, Silver 🥈, or Bronze 🥉 medals.

Click Next Step to try your first level!

steps.title: Level 1 — Your First Light
steps.content:
Level 1 — Your First Light

PyBot starts on the left. The glowing tile is at the far right. Walk forward to reach it, then call light().

Try writing the code yourself — use walk_forward() once for each tile, then light() at the end.

💡 Hint: You need to walk 4 tiles then light up. That's 4 walk_forward() calls then one light().

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Writing Less Code with Loops
steps.content:
That was a lot of typing! 🤔

If the light was 20 tiles away, you'd need to write walk_forward() twenty times. That gets boring fast!

A for loop tells Python to repeat something:

for i in range(4):
    walk_forward()

This does exactly the same as writing walk_forward() four times — but in just two lines!

💡 Remember the indent! The walk_forward() line is pushed in with spaces. This tells Python it belongs inside the loop.

On the next level, try to get the Gold medal by using a loop.

steps.title: Level 2 — Use a Loop!
steps.content:
Level 2 — Use a Loop!

This time the light is 5 tiles away. You could write walk_forward() five times — but to get Gold you'll need to use a loop!

Gold requires 3 lines or fewer. Can you do it?

💡 Hint: for i in range(5): then walk_forward() indented below, then light() after the loop.

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Turning
steps.content:
Turning ↪

PyBot can also turn! turn_right() turns 90° clockwise. turn_left() turns 90° the other way.

walk_forward()
walk_forward()
turn_right()
walk_forward()

This walks two tiles forward, then turns right, then walks one more tile in the new direction.

⚠️ turning does not move PyBot — it just changes the direction they're facing. You still need walk_forward() after a turn.

Watch PyBot demonstrate below — it walks two tiles, turns right, walks two more, then lights up:

steps.title: Level 3 — Turn the Corner
steps.content:
Level 3 — Turn the Corner

There are now two lights to reach. One is to the right, one is below it. You'll need to walk, then turn, then walk again.

The route makes an L shape. Think about where to turn and when to use light().

💡 Hint: Walk right to the first light. Then turn_right() to face downward. Walk down to the second light.

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Loop + Turn Pattern
steps.content:
Combining loops and turns

If you need to walk several steps and then turn, you can put the walking in a loop:

for i in range(4):
    walk_forward()
turn_right()
for i in range(3):
    walk_forward()

This walks forward 4 times, turns right, then walks 3 more times — in just 7 lines instead of 9!

On the next level, use this technique to reach all the lights.

Here it is in action — two loops with a turn in between:

steps.title: Level 4 — L Shape with Loops
steps.content:
Level 4 — L Shape with Loops

A longer L-shape this time. Two lights are waiting at the bottom-right corner. Use two for loops — one for each straight section — to reach them efficiently.

💡 Hint: Walk 4 right using a loop, then turn_right(), then walk 2 down using a loop. Light up both tiles at the end.

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Going in a Square
steps.content:
Can PyBot walk in a square? □

A square has 4 sides. Each side is the same length. After each side, PyBot turns right.

Here's how it looks without a loop:

# Side 1
walk_forward()
walk_forward()
turn_right()
# Side 2
walk_forward()
walk_forward()
turn_right()
# Side 3
...and so on

That's very repetitive! Look at the pattern: walk twice, turn right — and you do that same thing 4 times.

That means you can use a loop! Try it on the next level.

Watch PyBot walk three sides of a square — each side is walk, walk, light, turn:

steps.title: Level 5 — Walk in a Square
steps.content:
Level 5 — Walk in a Square

There are lights at three corners of a square. Walk around the square and light them all up.

Use a loop that repeats 3 times (one for each corner with a light): walk forward twice, light up, then turn right.

💡 Hint: Try: for side in range(3): walk_forward(), walk_forward(), light(), turn_right()

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Nested Loops
steps.content:
A loop inside a loop!

What if you need to walk 3 steps on each side of a square? You could write walk_forward() three times inside the loop — but there's an even better way.

You can put a loop inside another loop. This is called a nested loop:

for side in range(4):
    for step in range(3):
        walk_forward()
    turn_right()

Let's trace through it:

  • The outer loop runs 4 times (one per side of the square)
  • Each time, the inner loop runs 3 times (3 steps forward)
  • Then PyBot turns right, ready for the next side

💡 Count the indents! The inner loop is indented once (inside outer), and walk_forward() is indented twice (inside inner). turn_right() is indented once — inside outer but after inner.

Watch the nested loop in action — the inner loop handles the steps, the outer loop handles the sides:

steps.title: Level 6 — Nested Loop Square
steps.content:
Level 6 — Nested Loop Square

PyBot starts at the bottom-left corner facing up. There are lights at three other corners. Use a nested loop to walk 3 steps on each of the 3 lit sides.

The pattern is: walk 3 steps, light up, turn right — do that 3 times.

💡 Hint: Outer loop 3 times. Inner loop 3 times with walk_forward(). Then light() and turn_right() inside the outer loop.

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Challenge 1 — Bigger Square
steps.content:
Challenge 1 — Bigger Square

Challenge 1 🏆

A bigger grid this time! Three lights are waiting at corners of a 5×5 square. Can you work out how many steps each side needs?

Try to get Gold by keeping your solution to 4 lines or fewer.

🤔 Think first: the grid is 5×5. PyBot starts in a corner. Each side has 4 steps to the next corner. How do you change the inner loop?

💡 Hint: Change the inner range() to match the new step count. The rest of the structure stays the same!

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Challenge 2 — Full Perimeter
steps.content:
Challenge 2 — Full Perimeter

Challenge 2 🔥

Now light up the entire perimeter of a 4×4 grid — all 4 corners need lighting. PyBot starts at the bottom-left facing up.

This time you need to go around all 4 sides — including coming back to where you started. The start tile also needs a light!

💡 Start with light() before the loop, then loop 4 times.

💡 Hint: Light the starting tile first. Then loop 4 times: walk 3 steps, light, turn right.

🤖 PyBot is loading…

steps.title: Endless Mode
steps.content:
Endless Mode ∞

Keep practising! Each level gets a little harder. Solve it and a new one appears. Try to get Gold on every one!

Streak: 0
Best: 0
